fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the Marpit slide renderer wired up properly. Basically authors are writing their decks in Markdown and we need to turn that into proper HTML + CSS output. The core thing is splitting markdown into slides, handling those presenter-only notes (you know, the ones that shouldn't show up on screen), and making sure all the metadata stuff propagates correctly across slides like we discussed in the planning session.

Also there's the image handling — people want to do things like set background images and resize stuff inline using that alt-text trick, similar to how the image pipeline in the old asset module works. We need that supported.

On the CSS side, we need themes to be packaged up correctly — imports rolled up, scoped styles handled, and print rules included. The inline SVG wrapping mode also needs to work when enabled, and the section selectors need to be rewritten accordingly for that mode.

One more thing: the heading-based slide splitting needs to respect both numeric and array configs, and there's a directive override from within the document itself that should take priority. The header/footer repeat behavior also needs to work — inline markdown inside those values should render properly.

Let's make sure the output format matches exactly what downstream consumers expect. There are some edge cases around empty docs and code blocks that we got burned by before — make sure those are covered.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: render(markdown: string, options?: RenderOptions): { html: string | string[], css: string, comments: string[][] }

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
An empty Markdown string must still produce exactly one slide section. The output HTML should be `
` and comments should be `[[]]`. Never return zero slides for an empty input.

HTML comments that appear inside fenced code blocks or inline code spans must NOT be extracted as presenter comments. They should remain as literal code text in the rendered HTML. Only comments in normal prose or heading content are treated as presenter notes and stripped from the HTML output.

Directives prefixed with an underscore (e.g., `_class`) apply ONLY to the slide on which they are defined. Non-prefixed local directives (e.g., `class`) apply to the defining slide AND all subsequent slides. Global/deck-wide directives like `theme` propagate to all slides regardless of position.

The `headingDivider` option accepts either a number (split at that level and above) or an array of numbers (split only at exactly those heading levels). When set to `false` or an invalid value, no heading-based splitting occurs and all content stays on one slide. A document-level directive inside the Markdown can override the programmatic setting.

trigger_keywords: theme CSS path when inlineSVG enabled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When `inlineSVG` is enabled, all CSS selectors targeting `section` must be rewritten to `div.marpit > svg > foreignObject > section`. The default selector path without SVG is `div.marpit > section`. This rewriting must apply to base layout rules, normalization rules, theme CSS, print media rules, and advanced background rules.

Images whose alt text contains `bg` are treated as slide background directives. They set `background-image`, `background-color`, and `background-size` on the slide section and are completely suppressed from normal `` output. Regular images with only dimension options like `w:100` or `h:200` remain as visible `` elements with corresponding inline styles (unitless numbers get `px` appended).

Inline `<style>` elements are stripped from slide HTML and their content is appended to the generated CSS output. Scoped `<style scoped>` blocks additionally add a generated data attribute to the containing slide's `<section>` element and rewrite all CSS selectors in that block to scope them to that specific slide. Imports of registered themes within style blocks are suppressed as comments rather than active imports.

Header directive values are rendered as inline Markdown before insertion. A `<header>` element is inserted as the FIRST child of each affected slide section, and a `<footer>` element is inserted as the LAST child. If the directive value is not a string/text type, it is silently ignored and no header or footer element is produced for that slide.

trigger_keywords: theme name annotation in CSS comment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A theme stylesheet is identified by a `/* @theme theme-name */` comment. Parsing extracts the theme name, any `@import` references to other themes, and optional size properties (`width`, `height`). If the `@theme` annotation is absent the theme is considered invalid/unnamed. Absolute CSS units in size properties are converted to pixel values.

When packaging a theme for output, `@import` statements referencing other registered themes are resolved and their CSS is inlined in dependency order. Circular imports must be detected and raise an error rather than causing infinite recursion. Caller-supplied CSS in the `before` option is prepended to the packaged output. The `section` selector is used as the base selector in non-SVG packaged output. Invalid or unrecognized CSS snippets in caller-supplied CSS are silently ignored.

trigger_keywords: array output mode one item per slide
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When array output is requested, the renderer returns one HTML fragment string per slide instead of a single combined string. The `html_is_array` flag in output is `true` and `html_item_count` equals the number of slides. Each array item contains the markup for exactly one `<section>`. The wrapper `<div class="marpit">` is NOT included per-item in array mode.

trigger_keywords: YAML front matter strict vs loose
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In loose YAML mode, color-like values (e.g., CSS hex colors like `#fff`) that would be rejected by strict YAML parsing are accepted as unquoted strings in front matter and directive comments. Strict mode (default) requires proper YAML quoting for such values. The mode is configurable per render call.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: Image alt text is parsed for space-separated keyword tokens like `w:100`, `h:200`, `bg`, and color-like strings. These tokens control inline style, background directives, or text color rather than appearing as visible alt text. See feature6_images_and_backgrounds.json test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from the old asset module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The image pipeline parsing logic in the Marpit image plugin that interprets alt-text tokens for width (`w:`), height (`h:`), background keyword (`bg`), and color shorthand, converting them to CSS inline styles or slide-level directives.

context_pointers.vague_reference: those presenter-only notes that shouldn't show up on screen
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
HTML comments (`<!-- ... -->`) in Markdown prose are extracted as presenter comments into the `comments` array per slide and stripped from rendered HTML. The extraction is feature2_comments behavior — multi-line comments are normalized with `
` separators and the leading/trailing dashes of malformed comments are trimmed.

context_pointers.vague_reference: metadata stuff propagates correctly across slides
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Slide directives set via front matter or `<!-- directive: value -->` comments. Global directives (theme, headingDivider) apply deck-wide. Local directives (class, color, backgroundColor) apply from the defining slide forward. Underscore-prefixed variants (_class, _color) apply only to the current slide. See feature3_directives.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: downstream consumers expect exact output format
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The text-based output format used in test cases: newline-delimited key=value lines where `html`, `css`, `comments` are serialized fields. `comments` is JSON-encoded array of arrays. `html_is_array`, `html_item_count`, `section_count` are always present. The `css` field only appears when `include_css: true` is set in the input.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific details on right now. Could you look at the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` and the PRD in `start.md` for the relevant feature? The expected output format in the JSON test cases is the authoritative contract for that behavior.

feature10_theme_catalog.json.description: Manage a catalog of named themes, including existence checks, deletion, fallback selection, imported metadata resolution, imported size-property resolution, and circular-import errors.

themes=["test1","test2"]
check_0=true
check_1=true
check_2=false
check_3=true
final_size=1

feature1_slide_rendering.json.description: Render Markdown slide content into section-based HTML, preserving slide boundaries and optionally returning one HTML item per slide.

feature7_inline_svg_backgrounds.json.description: When inline SVG rendering is enabled, wrap slide content in SVG/foreignObject markup and render advanced backgrounds as separate background figures.

feature8_inline_styles.json.description: Remove embedded style elements from slide HTML, append their CSS to rendered stylesheet output, support scoped inline styles, and suppress valid theme imports.

feature9_theme_parsing.json.description: Parse theme stylesheets into named themes with metadata, imports, slide size properties, pixel conversions for absolute units, and normalized failures for missing required metadata.
